name: Hemoglobin A1c (HbA1c)
summary: HbA1c measures the percentage of hemoglobin that has glucose attached to it, reflecting average blood sugar over roughly the prior 2–3 months. It is a standard test for screening and monitoring diabetes.
whatItMeans: Because red blood cells live about three months, HbA1c integrates blood glucose over that window rather than at a single moment. By the American Diabetes Association, an HbA1c below 5.7% is normal, 5.7–6.4% is in the prediabetes range, and 6.5% or higher indicates the diabetes range. Higher values generally mean higher average glucose exposure and greater long-term complication risk. This is educational information, not a diagnosis.
raises: Chronically elevated blood glucose / insulin resistance and diabetes
Diet high in refined carbohydrates and sugar
Physical inactivity and excess body weight
GH-axis peptides such as tesamorelin, somatropin, cjc-1295, and ipamorelin, which can worsen glucose tolerance
lowers: Weight loss and lower-glycemic, higher-fiber diet
GLP-1 / GIP receptor agonists such as tirzepatide, retatrutide, and cagrilintide
Conditions that shorten red-cell lifespan (hemolysis, recent blood loss) can falsely lower it
confounders: Anemias and hemoglobinopathies (e.g. sickle cell trait, thalassemia) can falsely raise or lower HbA1c depending on red-cell turnover
Recent transfusion, hemolysis, or significant blood loss distorts results
Iron, B12, or folate deficiency can shift values
Pregnancy and chronic kidney disease alter red-cell lifespan and accuracy
Does not capture short-term glucose swings or hypoglycemia

ranges.note: Normal <5.7%; prediabetes 5.7–6.4%; diabetes ≥6.5% (American Diabetes Association). Lower bound shown as an approximate typical floor.

references.title: Understanding A1C Test | American Diabetes Association
Diabetes Diagnosis & Tests | American Diabetes Association
